name: Timed Challenge
description: A challenge starts from a defined trigger and requires success before a visible deadline expires.
design_notes: Timed challenges need a single authority for start, pause, checkpoint restoration, and failure. The player should know which event begins the countdown, whether menus or cutscenes suspend it, how grace windows are applied, and whether retries restore the original checkpoint_time_state or continue from the current world state.
design_purpose: Create urgency around traversal, puzzle solving, combat waves, or objective routing.
Measure mastery by asking players to execute a known route under pressure.
Turn otherwise safe content into a temporary commitment with readable stakes.
player_fantasy: The player feels the pressure of a deadline and the satisfaction of beating it cleanly.

parameters.name: timer_start_event_id
parameters.description: Event that begins the countdown and records the start timestamp for objective validation.

parameters.name: challenge_limit_ms
parameters.description: Allowed countdown window before the challenge resolves as failed or moves to a penalty state.

parameters.name: grace_window_ms
parameters.description: Small authority-side tolerance used to absorb latency, animation locks, or final-frame objective contact.

parameters.name: checkpoint_restore_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for what happens to countdown state when the player dies, reloads, or retries from a checkpoint.

parameters.name: failure_rule_id
parameters.description: Outcome applied when the authority clock passes the deadline without a success event.

edge_cases: The player pauses, opens a dialogue, or triggers a cutscene while the challenge is already active.
A success event and expiry event occur in the same simulation tick or arrive in different orders over the network.
Checkpoint reload restores player position but not spawned enemies, doors, or collected objective items.
The countdown UI rounds up while the authority deadline has already passed.
common_bugs: Start triggers fire multiple times and reset the countdown after the player has already begun the route.
Failure logic runs on the client and server, duplicating penalties, rewards, or reset effects.
The timer keeps advancing during loading screens because it reads a wall clock instead of the gameplay clock.
Countdown text, audio ticks, and objective state use different rounding rules near zero.
balancing_notes: The best limit usually gives skilled players room for one small mistake while still rewarding route knowledge.
If the challenge is required for progression, provide retry speed and checkpoint placement that respects repeated attempts.
Optional medal tiers can be stricter than the completion deadline without blocking less precise players.
accessibility_notes: Expose countdown information through text, icon state, and optional audio cues rather than relying only on color or flashing.
Offer relaxed limits or assist modes for players who need more planning or input execution time.

implementation_notes.unity: Store timer_start_event_id, started_at_ms, challenge_limit_ms, and checkpoint_restore_policy_id in the objective state. Use a gameplay clock for authority, unscaled UI only for display interpolation, and ensure OnTriggerEnter or input callbacks cannot restart an active challenge unless the state machine allows it.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent the challenge as a stateful Node or Resource with explicit start, success, expiry, and checkpoint restore transitions. Use _physics_process for authority ticks, emit countdown_changed for Control nodes, and serialize start tick plus remaining_ms for save/load.
implementation_notes.unreal: An Actor Component or Gameplay Ability task can own the countdown transaction. In networked play, record server timestamp at start, compare success and expiry on the server, replicate remaining time to UMG, and apply grace_window_ms before failing the objective.
implementation_notes.web: Keep countdown state in the simulation model and derive display text from remaining_ms. Browser pause, hidden tabs, and route changes should either suspend through pause_policy_id or restore from checkpoint_time_state rather than consuming wall-clock gaps.

example_games.title: Super Mario Bros.
example_games.note: Uses level timers to add pressure to platforming routes.

example_games.title: Spelunky
example_games.note: Uses escalating pressure when players spend too long in a level.
